content.text: WS-Federation (Web Services Federation) is a Microsoft-originated identity federation standard from 2003, part of the broader WS-* (WS-Star) family of SOAP/XML web service specifications. It defines how a browser-based client (passive profile) or a thick client (active profile via WS-Trust) obtains a security token from a Security Token Service (STS) and presents it to a Relying Party (RP) application.

Entra ID acts as a WS-Federation-compatible STS. The protocol is primarily used by older ASP.NET applications that relied on Windows Identity Foundation (WIF) or on-premises AD FS for SSO before OIDC matured. WS-Federation remains supported in Entra ID for backward compatibility, but all new applications should use OIDC instead.

attrs.alt: WS-Federation passive profile sign-in flow showing the 8-step sequence between user/browser, relying party, and Entra ID as STS, plus the WS-* protocol stack

content.text: WS-Federation and SAML both exchange signed XML security tokens, and in Entra ID the token inside a WS-Federation RSTR (RequestSecurityTokenResponse) is actually a SAML 1.1 or SAML 2.0 assertion. The protocols differ primarily in how the token exchange is initiated and framed — WS-Federation is the envelope/transport; SAML is the assertion format inside.
Do not use for new apps
If you encounter WS-Federation in an existing codebase, plan a migration to OIDC. Both protocols coexist in Entra ID, so migration can be done app-by-app without disrupting the identity tenant.
Entra ID WS-Federation Endpoints and Metadata

Entra ID exposes its WS-Federation configuration in a Federation Metadata Document — an XML file that relying parties consume to discover endpoints and signing certificates automatically. The ASP.NET WsFederation middleware reads this document at startup.

# WS-Federation metadata endpoints for Entra ID
# The Federation Metadata Document describes the STS: endpoints, signing certs, claims

TENANT_ID = "<your-tenant-id>"

# Tenant-specific metadata:
metadata_url = f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/federationmetadata/2007-06/federationmetadata.xml"

# v2 endpoint (preferred):
v2_metadata = f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration"
# (The OIDC discovery doc also serves as the authoritative endpoint list for Entra)

# WS-Federation sign-in endpoint:
wsfed_endpoint = f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/wsfed"

# WS-Trust 1.3 endpoint (active profile — for thick clients):
wstrust_endpoint = f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/wstrust/issue/usernamemixed"

import requests
# Fetch and inspect the federation metadata
resp = requests.get(metadata_url)
print('Content-Type:', resp.headers.get('Content-Type'))
print('Size:', len(resp.content), 'bytes')

# The XML contains:
# - EntityDescriptor with entityID = https://sts.windows.net/{tenant-id}/
# - SPSSODescriptor (Entra as IdP for SAML)
# - RoleDescriptor for WS-Federation passiveSTS
# - KeyDescriptor with X509Certificate (signing key)
# - PassiveRequestorEndpoint (the wsfed endpoint URL)
The WS-Federation sign-in endpoint is https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/wsfed — POST-based token delivery goes to the same URL
wtrealm: the RP's identifier (equivalent to SAML Entity ID or OIDC client_id) — must match the app registration's identifier URI
wctx: opaque context value the RP passes in; Entra returns it unchanged in the wresult — used to carry session state (similar to OAuth state)
wreply: optional redirect URL after sign-in; must match a Reply URL registered in the Entra app

The `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.WsFederation` package provides middleware that handles the WS-Federation passive profile: redirecting unauthenticated users to Entra, consuming the POST-ed RSTR, validating the embedded SAML assertion, and establishing a cookie session. The Wtrealm value must match the app's Identifier URI in Entra.
The WsFederation middleware automatically reads the signing certificate from the MetadataAddress — no manual cert management needed if the metadata URL is accessible
ClaimsPrincipal.Claims contains the attribute claims from the SAML assertion inside the RSTR, mapped via the WIF claims namespace URIs (e.g., ClaimTypes.Name, ClaimTypes.Email)
Set SaveTokens = true to store the raw RSTR in the authentication properties — needed when the token must be passed downstream to a WCF service
For sign-out, call SignOutAsync on both the Cookie and WsFederation schemes — the WsFederation handler builds the wsignout1.0 redirect
WS-Trust Active Profile for Thick Clients

The WS-Trust active profile allows a non-browser application (WPF desktop app, console script, WCF service client) to obtain a security token by directly POST-ing credentials to the STS SOAP endpoint. This bypasses the browser redirect flow. MSAL's AcquireTokenByUsernamePassword uses WS-Trust internally when the tenant uses federated authentication.
WS-Trust / ROPC (Resource Owner Password Credentials) cannot satisfy MFA — Conditional Access policies requiring MFA will block it
For managed (non-federated) Entra accounts, MSAL falls back to the native OIDC ROPC grant rather than WS-Trust
Federated domains (AD FS) use WS-Trust 1.3: MSAL sends the username/password to the on-premises AD FS WS-Trust endpoint, which issues a token that MSAL then exchanges with Entra
WS-Trust active profile is discouraged for new development — it exposes passwords to the client application, defeating the purpose of federated identity

WS-Federation applications in Entra ID can be migrated to OIDC without changing the underlying user identities. The migration involves updating the application's authentication middleware, changing the Entra app registration's SSO mode, and adding OIDC redirect URIs. Because Entra maintains the same user object IDs across protocol changes, existing user records remain valid.
IdP WS-Fed config in vendor portal

WS-Federation tokens carry claims using the older WS-Federation claim type URIs (long URN strings). In .NET, these map to `ClaimTypes` constants. The claim set Entra includes depends on the app's optional claims configuration — the same manifest settings that control OIDC and SAML claims also affect WS-Federation RSTR content.
The object identifier (`oid`) claim is the stable user identifier in Entra — use it as the user's primary key in your database, not the UPN which can change
Group membership appears as individual claims with the group OID as the value — for >150 groups, Entra omits groups and includes a hasgroups=true claim instead
The authnmethodsreferences claim indicates which authentication methods were used (pwd, mfa, rsa, etc.) — useful for step-up authentication logic
WS-Federation claim URIs and OIDC claim names differ: prefer OIDC standard claim names when possible, since they are shorter and consistent across providers
WS-Federation with Federated Domains and AD FS

Before Entra native authentication became capable, organizations configured their domains as 'federated' against on-premises AD FS. In this setup, Entra acts as a passthrough — it redirects authentication to AD FS, which authenticates the user using Kerberos or NTLM against Active Directory, issues a WS-Federation token, and sends it back to Entra. Entra then issues its own token to the RP.

This double-hop WS-Federation chain (App → Entra → AD FS → Entra → App) is the original hybrid identity architecture. Many organizations are migrating away from it by converting federated domains to managed authentication with Password Hash Sync.

# WS-Federation with on-premises AD FS (federated domain scenario)
# When a domain is federated with AD FS, Entra redirects authentication to AD FS

# Check if a domain is federated:
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Domain.Read.All"
$domain = Get-MgDomain -DomainId "contoso.com"
Write-Host "Authentication type: $($domain.authenticationType)"  # 'Managed' or 'Federated'

# Get federation settings for a domain:
$federation = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/domains/contoso.com/federationConfiguration"
$federation.value | Select-Object issuerUri, passiveSignInUri, activeSignInUri, signingCertificate

# Federated sign-in flow with WS-Fed:
# 1. User types alice@contoso.com in Entra sign-in page
# 2. Entra detects 'contoso.com' is federated
# 3. Entra redirects to AD FS (passiveSignInUri) with wa=wsignin1.0&wtrealm=urn:federation:MicrosoftOnline
# 4. AD FS authenticates the user (Kerberos/NTLM/smart card) and issues a WS-Fed token
# 5. AD FS posts the token back to Entra
# 6. Entra validates the token and issues its own Entra token to the RP

# Convert a federated domain back to managed (remove AD FS dependency):
# WARNING: This removes AD FS as the authentication path for ALL users on this domain.
# Ensure all users have cloud passwords (PHS enabled) before running.
$update = @{ authenticationType = "Managed" }
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/domains/contoso.com" \
    -Body ($update | ConvertTo-Json)
AD FS MFA Adapter or Entra MFA after federation

Microsoft recommends converting federated domains to managed authentication (PHS + Seamless SSO) to eliminate the AD FS infrastructure dependency and gain full Entra Conditional Access, Identity Protection, and passwordless authentication support.

WS-Federation failures are debugged by inspecting the raw wresult XML posted to the RP and correlating error codes in the Entra sign-in logs. The wresult contains the full RequestSecurityTokenResponse, including the embedded SAML assertion and any error details if sign-in failed at the STS.

# Debugging WS-Federation authentication issues

# 1. Decode a wresult (RSTR) from the browser's POST body
# Capture with Fiddler, browser DevTools, or a local proxy
import urllib.parse, xml.dom.minidom

def decode_wresult(raw_wresult: str) -> str:
    """URL-decode and pretty-print the wresult XML."""
    decoded = urllib.parse.unquote(raw_wresult)
    return xml.dom.minidom.parseString(decoded.encode()).toprettyxml(indent='  ')

# The wresult is an XML RequestSecurityTokenResponse containing:
# - wst:Lifetime (token validity window)
# - wsp:AppliesTo (the RP's wtrealm)
# - wst:RequestedSecurityToken > saml:Assertion (the actual identity claims)

# 2. Common WS-Federation errors from Entra:

# AADSTS50011: 'The reply URL does not match'
# -> wreply in the request doesn't match a registered Reply URL
#    Check Entra app registration → Authentication → Reply URLs

# AADSTS50001: 'The application was not found in the directory'
# -> wtrealm doesn't match any app registration's Identifier URI
#    Ensure the Identifier URI in the app registration exactly matches wtrealm

# AADSTS650056: 'Misconfigured application — missing redirect URI'
# -> No Reply URL configured in the app registration

# 3. Enable debug logging in ASP.NET Core WsFederation middleware:
# In appsettings.Development.json:
# { "Logging": { "LogLevel": {
#     "Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication": "Debug",
#     "Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.WsFederation": "Trace"
# }}}

# 4. Use SAML-tracer browser extension:
# Works for WS-Federation too (the wresult field contains XML)
# Shows wa, wtrealm, wreply, wctx parameters and the raw wresult

# 5. Check Entra sign-in logs:
# Azure AD > Sign-in logs > search by Application or CorrelationId from error page
# The 'Authentication Protocol' column shows 'WsFed' for WS-Federation sign-ins
WS-Federation sign-in appears in Entra sign-in logs with Authentication Protocol = 'WsFed' — correlate by CorrelationId from the error page
Mismatch between wtrealm and the app's Identifier URI is the most common configuration error — they must be identical strings

If the WS-Federation middleware throws 'Unable to validate token', check that the federation metadata URL is accessible from the web server and the signing certificate in metadata matches what Entra is currently using
Certificate rotation: when Entra rotates its signing certificate, the metadata URL is updated automatically — but if you hard-coded the certificate, you'll need to update it manually
Monitoring WS-Federation Usage and Planning Migration

Understanding which applications still use WS-Federation — and how actively — is the first step in a migration plan. Entra ID's sign-in logs record the authentication protocol for every sign-in, making it straightforward to inventory active WS-Federation applications and prioritize migration by usage volume.

Applications with low or zero WS-Federation sign-in volume in the past 90 days are lower-risk migration candidates. High-volume applications should be migrated carefully with parallel testing (run OIDC alongside WS-Federation during a transition period if the SP supports both).

# Monitor WS-Federation sign-ins via Microsoft Graph audit logs
# WS-Federation sign-ins appear in the auditLogs/signIns endpoint

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "AuditLog.Read.All"

# Get recent WS-Federation sign-ins
$filter = [System.Uri]::EscapeDataString(
    "authenticationProtocol eq 'wsFed' and createdDateTime ge " +
    (Get-Date).AddDays(-7).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
)
$signIns = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns?" + '$filter=' + $filter + "&" + '$top=100' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

$signIns | Select-Object ` 
    createdDateTime,
    userPrincipalName,
    appDisplayName,
    authenticationProtocol,
    @{N="Status"; E={ if ($_.status.errorCode -eq 0) 'Success' else "Error: $($_.status.failureReason)" }},
    ipAddress

# Count WS-Fed apps still actively used (identify migration candidates)
$wsFedApps = $signIns | Group-Object appDisplayName | Sort-Object Count -Descending
Write-Host 'WS-Federation apps with sign-ins in last 7 days:'
$wsFedApps | Select-Object Name, Count | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Apps with no sign-ins in 90 days can be decommissioned or migrated with lower urgency
$cutoff = (Get-Date).AddDays(-90)
$old90 = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri ("https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns" +
          "?" + '$filter=' + [System.Uri]::EscapeDataString(
              "authenticationProtocol eq 'wsFed' and createdDateTime ge " +
              $cutoff.ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')) +
          "&" + '$count=true&$top=1') |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty '@odata.count'
Write-Host "WS-Fed sign-ins in last 90 days: $old90"
Target zero active WS-Federation applications as a security hardening goal — reduces reliance on XML signature stacks and SOAP-era XML parsers
For applications where the vendor controls the SP (SaaS), raise a support ticket asking the vendor to add OIDC/SAML support if they only support WS-Federation
During migration, run both WS-Federation and OIDC configurations in parallel for a test period — Entra supports having both on the same app registration
After migrating, disable (not delete) the WS-Federation app registration for 30 days before removing — ensures no undocumented integrations were relying on it

Microsoft has not announced a deprecation timeline for WS-Federation in Entra ID, but it is in maintenance mode. No new WS-Federation features are being added. New investments (Continuous Access Evaluation, Conditional Access v2, passwordless) require OIDC.

WS-Federation is a legacy XML-based federation protocol supported by Entra ID for backward compatibility with ASP.NET/.NET Framework applications and AD FS integrations. The protocol delivers a SAML assertion to browser-based relying parties via an HTTP POST after the user authenticates at Entra.
WS-Federation is maintained in Entra ID but should not be used for new applications — use OIDC instead
The token inside a WS-Federation RSTR is a SAML assertion — WS-Federation is the envelope; SAML is the assertion format
Configure via MetadataAddress (federation metadata XML) and Wtrealm (identifier URI) in ASP.NET middleware
WS-Trust active profile (thick clients) cannot satisfy MFA and is deprecated for new scenarios
Migration path: ASP.NET Core apps swap WsFederation middleware for AddOpenIdConnect with minimal code changes
